content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Supply-side contraction of the selective track (shrink the gate, not the rules)'. Early selection at age 10–12 into parallel 6- and 8-year grammar schools is the primary structural cause of Hungary's current position as Europe's most socially selective education system and should be phased out by 2035 in favor of single-entry-age (14 or 15) and unified secondary-school admission to reduce inequality of opportunity without sacrificing demonstrable value-added in pedagogy.
opportunities: Shrinks the selective track's footprint without requiring statutory redefinition of the secondary-education age boundary, potentially avoiding the felmenő-rendszer and ~1-year lead-time obligations that a structural reform would trigger.

Aligns policy with the demographic reality that the exam's original rationing function is eroding, as evidenced by falling 8-year-track applications (-14% year-on-year) alongside flat overall enrolment.
strengths: Approximately 8–12% of a Hungarian age cohort enters 6- or 8-year grammar school (gimnazium) tracks, selected at age 10 or 12 largely via central written examination; entrants are strongly skewed toward high-SES, urban families.

Since 2022, Hungary has become Europe's most socially selective school system according to PISA; Hungary's social selectivity index worsened by 5.3 points between 2018 and 2022, the largest deterioration in Europe alongside Poland's tracking re-introduction.
threats: Reversal requires legislative repeal since score thresholds are maintainer-level administrative practice rather than statute, offering more procedural friction than S1's decree-based mechanism, but a parliamentary majority change during the multi-year rollout still poses a significant reversal risk given the Fidesz framing of any selection-related change and the government's own pattern of rapidly reversing predecessor reforms.

Church and foundation maintainers, protected by Tisza's coalition commitment to preserve prior operating conditions, may simply expand to absorb displaced demand, neutralizing the intended contraction and creating a policy-credibility problem.
weaknesses: What is the true causal magnitude of peer effects vs. pedagogical quality in the observed 10% value-added in elite tracks? Current research separates them only weakly.
How much of the performance gap between grammar and vocational students would narrow if school choice and catchment sorting were held constant and only early selection were removed?
